issues.label: Weekly edition
issues.title: Mac utility week: remote displays, calmer audio and a Dock that stays put
issues.dek: Ten useful Mac developments, led by a polished remote display app and a cluster of small utilities that solve very specific annoyances.
intro: The first proper edition of Mac App Digest leans into the best kind of Mac software: focused tools that fix one recurring frustration without asking you to rebuild your workflow.

This week’s list includes new and recently surfaced indie apps, one notable consumer AI update and a quick platform watch. Each item includes the reason it may be useful plus the tradeoff worth knowing before you install it.

apps.name: Mirage
apps.tagline: Stream a Mac window or full desktop to another Apple device.
body: Mirage can send individual Mac apps, a complete desktop or a secondary display to an iPhone, iPad, Vision Pro or another Mac. It supports hardware-accelerated video, dynamic ProMotion frame rates and Apple Pencil input, with remote connections protected by media encryption.
apps.why: It is a much more flexible idea than basic screen mirroring, especially if you want to place one Mac app on an iPad or reach a headless Mac from another room.
apps.caveat: The receiving app needs the free Mirage Host on the Mac. Streaming up to eight windows and some remote-access features require Pro.

apps.name: DockFreeze
apps.tagline: Stop the Dock from wandering across multiple displays.
body: DockFreeze is a lightweight menu bar app that locks the macOS Dock to the display you choose. Its entire job is preventing the Dock from jumping to another monitor when the pointer hits the bottom edge.
apps.why: Multi-monitor users get a permanent fix for a small but surprisingly persistent macOS irritation.
apps.caveat: This is intentionally a single-purpose utility. If your Dock already behaves, there is nothing else here to justify installing it.

apps.name: Auto Ducking
apps.tagline: Lower background audio automatically when your microphone turns on.
body: Auto Ducking watches for microphone activity, lowers music, video or ambient audio to a level you choose, then restores the previous volume when the microphone is no longer in use. It is designed for calls, dictation, recording and voice-based AI tools.
apps.why: It removes the repeated pause-and-resume step from the growing number of Mac workflows that use a microphone throughout the day.
apps.caveat: It includes a seven-day trial, so continued use is paid. Test it with your particular audio and microphone apps before committing.

apps.name: Docomments
apps.tagline: Attach Markdown notes directly to Mac files and folders.
body: Docomments adds a floating note to any file or folder. Notes support Markdown, save into the file’s metadata, appear in Finder’s Get Info window and remain searchable through Spotlight. Files can be added by drag and drop, a global shortcut or Finder Services.
apps.why: It creates a lightweight layer for change notes, context and reminders without renaming files or maintaining a separate database.
apps.caveat: Because the comments rely on file metadata, test whether they survive the cloud sync, archive or transfer tools you use most often.

apps.name: Mailway
apps.tagline: Route every mailto link to the right email app or webmail service.
body: Mailway becomes the Mac’s default email handler, then redirects mailto links according to rules for the recipient, domain or subdomain. That makes it possible to send work addresses to one account and personal addresses to another without changing defaults first.
apps.why: It solves a real problem for anyone who splits email between Gmail, another webmail account and native Mac apps.
apps.caveat: Routing is automatic rather than a chooser that appears after every click. Good rules are essential, and Mailway must be set as the default email app.

apps.name: Mobile Builds
apps.tagline: Track Xcode Cloud builds without living in a browser tab.
body: Mobile Builds surfaces Xcode Cloud continuous-integration status, history, analytics and failure notifications on macOS and iOS. It is aimed at making build health understandable at a glance while developers stay inside their normal workflow.
apps.why: Independent Apple developers can see failed builds sooner and spend less time digging through the Xcode Cloud interface.
apps.caveat: Its value is tightly tied to Xcode Cloud. Developers using GitHub Actions, Bitrise or another CI service should skip it.

apps.name: Gemini Spark for Mac
apps.tagline: Google’s Mac assistant is beginning to act on files and connected apps.
body: Google is bringing Spark into the Gemini app for macOS with access to explicitly shared local files, real-time topic tracking, third-party app connections and support for Model Context Protocol integrations. The pitch moves Gemini beyond a chat window toward multi-step desktop work.
apps.why: A native Mac agent that can work with local files and external tools could become more useful than another shortcut to a chatbot.
apps.caveat: Spark is still a beta and initial availability is limited to Google AI Ultra subscribers in selected countries.

apps.name: SonicFlow
apps.tagline: Independent volume sliders for every app producing audio.
body: SonicFlow is a free, open-source per-app volume mixer for macOS. It provides separate sliders for apps such as Music, Zoom and Chrome, plus automatic ducking when someone speaks on a call. The developer says it uses under one percent CPU and includes no telemetry.
apps.why: Per-app audio control remains a conspicuous Mac omission, and SonicFlow packages the missing feature into a clean native panel.
apps.caveat: The current release is version 0.1. Treat it as promising early software and verify stability with your own audio setup.

apps.name: Dockspace
apps.tagline: Put widgets, previews and controls directly into the Mac Dock.
body: Dockspace extends the Dock with app previews, animations, music controls, calendars and other widgets. It is a visual, interaction-heavy alternative to utilities that simply rearrange or replace Dock icons.
apps.why: It treats the Dock as usable screen space rather than a static launcher, which could be handy on large displays.
apps.caveat: Dock customization apps can overlap or conflict. Test it without other Dock modifiers running before deciding whether it belongs in your setup.

apps.name: macOS 27 beta 3
apps.tagline: The next Mac release reaches its third developer beta.
body: Apple released macOS 27 beta 3 on July 6 alongside Xcode 27 beta 3 and the fourth macOS 26.6 beta. For app developers, this is another checkpoint for testing compatibility with the next generation of macOS before the wider public beta.
apps.why: The beta cycle determines when indie apps can safely adopt new platform features and when everyday users can expect compatibility work to accelerate.
apps.caveat: This remains developer beta software. It does not belong on a production Mac that you rely on every day.

issues.closing: That’s the week. The archive will keep this edition available while next Friday’s issue takes over the home page.

issues.label: From the editor
issues.title: Welcome to Mac App Digest
issues.dek: A short weekly field guide for finding Mac apps that are genuinely useful.
intro: There are more interesting Mac apps than anyone can reasonably track. Mac App Digest is a small weekly filter: a few utilities, thoughtful context and a clear sense of who each app is actually for.
The website keeps the latest edition easy to read while giving older issues a permanent home. Each issue has its own shareable link, works well on a phone and remains available without an attachment.

apps.name: The format
apps.tagline: Short enough to finish, useful enough to save.
body: Each edition focuses on a small group of apps rather than an exhaustive list. The goal is to explain what makes them interesting, where they fit and any tradeoffs worth knowing before you install them.
apps.verdict: A clean read with no attachment hunting and an archive that gets more useful every week.

issues.closing: Thanks for reading. The newest issue will always be waiting on the home page.
